You know a story that's like meaningful but there's plenty out there to pay attention to like good storytellers. But and don't take this wrong but because I'm not but the question is sort of the proverbial we want a simple answer to a complex problem. And there is no simple answer to the complex problem. You know. I guess what I was asking is have you seen like over the years particularly that out of say fascination or legend or whatever it is that when it's come down to this that you're seeing. Well yeah what we talked about earlier I mean look what the most important thing is that they believe you're a real human being. That probably is overall the most important thing. I mean fundamentally people buy things from people they like. Now you can they can dislike you and you can sell to them. There's a set of skills for that. You know you can bully them into a sale. You can intimidate them into a sale but keeping them over a period of time the people who sell those ways don't keep them. So you know well I'll pick on. So you can sell a lot of stuff being Joel Bauer but you ain't going to keep people around. Month after month after month year after year after year because you like knocked them down took their wallet out of their pocket took the money you know and popped them back up in the chair and they're dazed and confused and they don't know what the hell happened to them. Well I mean you can do that but it doesn't really serve our long term purposes. So ultimately people got to find a reason to like you and then if you will the fascination they got to have reasons to stay fascinated with you. It's almost like every town has a small little diner or restaurant you look at it and the person from not in that town would drive past not thinking it was anything but the people in that little town like it they go there they continue to go there maybe it's not the most perfect place it's kind of the cheers effect if you will. It usually does everything to do with food see it has to do with place. So that's their place. Think of the neighborhood tavern business I mean there's a million of them and they all have 22 regulars that support the whole deal. Right? Now eight of them are complete alchies but the rest I'm marked. You know that's their place and see again that's sort of like what we're trying to create and if you try and create a place for everybody it's a place nobody likes. Right? So you make some sacrifices along the way. So who, oh it was you. Captain Marvel whatever your current identity is you know like you said you sort of were headed in a direction of trying to be acceptable to everybody and are sort of rethinking that and you know I think that's right. You know so you know a definitive place is better than a non-definitive place because the people who connect with it really connect. So are lifers if you will which is admittedly a small percentage of the people who come through the system. Right? But are lifers have enormous value. You create a lifer because they really connect. Is there a difference in learning to speak with humor and writing with humor? Yeah. Yeah writing is harder because it's a one on one activity say people will laugh if other people around them laugh and sort of like there's in every group there's an easy buyer. You know there's the 5% who buy anything from anybody because they're a practitioner of a slight edge and they'll run back to the room there's like 5% of the people who are easy laughers. And so they'll like kind of start the thing. And you can create an environment of being an in fun environment where you can get that they're supposed to laugh in print. So like see if you look at the stories in the back of the notebook. And I didn't give you a lot of humorous ones but there's one or two in there and in the Turner stuff I'll get copied for you. Like the Butter story is supposed to be funny. It's supposed to get a laugh. Much harder to get a laugh with that story in print than it is personally. You can't do voice inflection, you can't do body language, there's no audience reinforcement right. It's why TV sitcoms have to have laugh tracks. Because you're sitting at a, some of them suck but even the good ones. Because you're sitting at home in a living room by yourself. Show with a live audience. See they don't need a laugh track for the live audience. The live audience laughs. So it's much harder to do it in print. The people who are true humorous writers think Thurber or like one of the best writers. They don't do it much anymore but if you go get all the Woody Allen's early stuff Woody's a fabulous humorous writer, a wordsmith kind of guy. And you'll sit there reading a Woody Allen collection of stories on an airplane and you'll laugh out loud. So like there's a story about a Jewish couple going to the party that they were dressed as a deer and then they hit a deer and they strap the deer on the car and it wakes up by the time they get to the party and the deer goes into the party and then the Mrs. Gafelda who's dressed as the deer wins the best costume prize and the deer's all pissed off because he didn't win the prize. It's a fabulous story. I mean you sit there and he's got like every word right, he's got every phrase right and you just sit there and laugh. Well that's really hard to do in print. And I don't think I achieve it. I think I achieve amusement. But so no it's easier to do it on stage. It's also by the way more essential to do it on stage. If you don't make them laugh every seven minutes on stage you're in trouble. Have you come across a good book? A book about humor? Yeah there's a number of them. Jarvis has a book, don't remember the title. Herb True has a book called Humor Power, don't remember the title. Steve Allen has two books, don't remember the titles. One is How to Be Funny by Steve Allen and they're very good. Steve's pretty analytical about the process. If you hand me a note or you fax me or something if you want, I mean I got a shelf of them. I'll jot them down and get them to you. Nightingale has a product, they don't put it in their catalog much I think, but I think they still have it. And it's like the treasury of humorous speakers or something like that. It's got 12 or 20 CDs in it. And it's all different humorous speakers. It's worth having. And I think from NSA you can still get Charlie Jarvis's old stuff. And there's a product called the Humor Workshop where he actually taught humor. Which is worth having. So I think NSA still has that stuff available. They just don't tell anybody. But I think they got it. I think Ed McMahon's got a public, yeah he does. He has a public speaking book that has a whole section in it about humor too. That's pretty good. Any other observations from going through everything? Ah-has? I have a separate question to ask you. I didn't ask you for a question. I asked you for an ah-ha. Alright, what about ahs I had? You don't use bullets in a lot of your copy. A lot of copywriters do. Is that because of rhythm? Yeah, it is. And I'm not a fan of the big long three pages of bullets in the middle of a sales letter. When I do it, you'll usually find me break it out. So in a direct mail package, you'll find a sales letter and you'll find the bullets somewhere else.
